label: Uneasy calm
summary: The world held in the same uneasy-calm band it has occupied all week, with siege, war and record heat pressing on one side and quiet science, diplomacy and compassion on the other. Iran's seven-day state funeral for slain former supreme leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei moved into its second day at Tehran's Grand Mosalla, with mourners chanting for revenge and delegations arriving as Washington and Tehran kept indirect denuclearization talks paused until July 11, a fragile breathing space rather than progress. Europe's deadliest conflict since 1945 intensified as Ukraine pressed a 40-day escalation, unleashing one of its largest drone assaults on Crimea and a dozen Russian regions while Kyiv still absorbed the aftermath of the July 2 strike that killed at least 22 people. In Sudan, UN human rights chief Volker Turk and Human Rights Watch warned of imminent mass atrocities around besieged El Obeid, where roughly half a million trapped civilians face killings, abductions and sexual violence and food prices have surged up to 300 percent. The humanitarian ledger stayed heavy: Venezuela's twin-quake toll reached about 2,595 dead with nearly 50,000 unaccounted for as hope of finding survivors faded into recovery, and the DR Congo Bundibugyo Ebola epidemic passed 450 deaths among more than 1,460 cases. Climate weighed on both hemispheres as a historic heat dome pushed life-threatening conditions onto more than 200 million Americans, forced Washington to cancel its July 4 parade at a forecast-record 101 degrees, and Europe braced for a third record heatwave. Against that ran genuine lift: on Independence Day Pope Leo XIV visited the migrant island of Lampedusa with a plea for compassion and integration; US markets closed the holiday week near records after a soft jobs report eased Fed-hike fears; and researchers reported CAR-T therapy eliminating glioblastoma in preclinical models, a near-100-fold gain in quantum magnon lifetimes, a new walking-shark species off Papua New Guinea, and data showing 800 million people have gained electricity access since 2010. The headline settles at 43, a notch below neutral, weighed by war, siege and heat even as science and public health quietly delivered.

drivers.headline: Iran's seven-day state funeral for slain former supreme leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei enters its second day at Tehran's Grand Mosalla, where his flag-draped casket is on public display and mourners chant for revenge, as officials forecast up to 15 to 20 million mourners across a week of processions to Qom and Karbala, while the United States and Iran keep indirect denuclearization talks paused until July 11 so negotiators can attend, a fragile diplomatic breathing space rather than a breakthrough

Ukraine presses a 40-day escalation ordered by President Volodymyr Zelenskyy, unleashing one of its heaviest drone bombardments since the invasion against Russian-held Crimea, the surrounding seas and a dozen Russian regions and striking two reconnaissance ships and a ferry, as Kyiv still absorbs the aftermath of the July 2 Russian missile and drone attack that killed at least 22 people and Moscow rejects limits on long-range strikes

UN human rights chief Volker Turk and Human Rights Watch warn of imminent large-scale atrocities around Sudan's besieged city of El Obeid, where the Rapid Support Forces have encircled roughly half a million trapped civilians facing killings, abductions and sexual violence, with UN offices documenting 15 drone strikes killing at least 45 people in three weeks and food prices surging up to 300 percent as Britain convenes an emergency Human Rights Council session

Venezuela's twin-earthquake death toll reaches about 2,595 with more than 12,400 injured and nearly 50,000 people still unaccounted for as hope of finding survivors fades into a recovery effort more than a week after the June 24 back-to-back magnitude-7.2 and 7.5 quakes near Caracas, with over 860 aftershocks recorded and satellite analysis estimating some 58,870 buildings damaged or destroyed

A historic heat dome pushes life-threatening conditions onto more than 200 million Americans through the July 4 holiday, with Washington forecast to hit a record 101 degrees Fahrenheit and its Independence Day parade canceled, New York expecting triple digits for the first time in a decade, and heat indices nearing 110 degrees across the eastern two-thirds of the country as Europe braces for a third record-shattering heatwave

China's military promotes two new full generals days after an anti-corruption purge thinned its top ranks, with Xi Jinping presenting orders on July 3 elevating Zhang Shuguang to lead the Central Military Commission's discipline body and Wang Gang to head the PLA Air Force, a shake-up widely read as an effort to secure the military's loyalty to the Communist Party amid probes that have felled former vice-chairmen Zhang Youxia and He Weidong

On Independence Day, Pope Leo XIV visits the Mediterranean migrant island of Lampedusa with a plea for compassion, praying at a migrant cemetery, touring the Door of Europe memorial and blessing a pier renamed for Pope Francis, urging Europe to receive, protect, support and integrate migrants and telling Americans that protecting human life includes welcoming immigrants

US markets close the holiday week near records after a soft June jobs report showing just 57,000 positions added, versus 113,000 expected, eased fears of a near-term Federal Reserve rate hike, sending the Dow to an all-time high on July 2 and the small-cap Russell 2000 to its best first half since 1991, with the market shut July 3 for Independence Day

The DR Congo Bundibugyo Ebola epidemic passes 450 deaths among more than 1,460 confirmed cases, spreading to North and South Kivu and reaching the major city of Kisangani some 600 kilometers from its Ituri epicenter, as health workers battle community mistrust and no vaccine exists for the strain, though clinical trials of the antibody MBP134 and antiviral remdesivir are now under way

Researchers report a run of medical and physics advances, with CAR-T cell therapy eliminating detectable glioblastoma, the deadliest brain cancer, in preclinical models in work led from King's College London, alongside a quantum breakthrough extending the lifetime of magnons nearly 100-fold to 18 microseconds, opening a path to using the tiny magnetic waves as carriers of quantum information

Global development data offers quiet encouragement, with roughly 800 million people having gained access to electricity since 2010 to reach a 92 percent global access rate in 2024, while air pollution keeps falling across Europe as sulphur and nitrogen oxide emissions drop 3 to 5 percent a year and scientists confirm a tenth walking-shark species, the Dudgeon walking shark, off Papua New Guinea

Australia deepens its Pacific engagement after signing the Nakamal Agreement with Vanuatu to bar foreign military bases and make security a shared Pacific responsibility, while Foreign Minister Penny Wong confirms a 2 million dollar Australian contribution toward emergency food, shelter, water, sanitation and psychosocial support for Venezuela's earthquake survivors

sources_consulted: Wikipedia (Timeline of the Russo-Ukrainian war / 2026 Venezuela earthquakes / 2026 Ebola epidemic / 2026 North American heat wave / 2026 European heatwaves / Gaza peace plan)
